data.title: EMEA Morning Briefing: Oil Climbs After Houthis Join Iran Conflict
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

EU business and consumer surveys; Germany CPI; U.K. money and credit; no major corporate trading updates expected

Opening Call:

European stock futures traded lower early Monday. Asian stock benchmarks were mostly lower; the dollar weakened; Treasury yields were down; while oil futures and gold gained.

Equities:

Stock futures point to a lower open in Europe, as investors are waking up to the reality that the economic effects of the Iran war-now entering its fifth week-are likely to last longer than first expected.

As a Marine Expeditionary Unit arrived in the Middle East-pushing the number of U.S. troops there to around 50,000-Iran warned on Sunday that it would "destroy" any American ground invasion. On Saturday, Yemen-based Houthi rebels said they had entered the conflict, firing missiles at Israel. That raises the threat of renewed missile attacks against shipping in the Red Sea.

"The Houthis' entering the theater reshapes the battlefield. What looked contained now resembles a fracture line running through the entire energy complex," said Stephen Innes, managing partner at SPI Asset Management.

Forex:

Barclays sees the dollar remaining supported by elevated energy prices in the near term, but expects it to weaken more broadly once tensions in the Middle East decline a few months down the line.

It estimates that every 10% increase in oil prices would give the dollar a 0.5% to 1.0% boost. However, "geopolitical stabilization within the next three months, a shift of focus towards the [U.S.] administration's domestic agenda and new Fed leadership likely imply a degree of softness in the near term, per our new forecasts," Barclays said. It sees the dollar at 1.18 euros in the next quarter.

Bonds:

Treasury yields were down, after jumping about 50 basis points in one month on worries about the inflationary impact of rising crude-oil prices, coupled with a series of disappointing U.S. debt auctions.

"There's really been no place to hide this month," said George Cipolloni, a veteran portfolio manager. "You can't go to stocks, you can't go to bonds-even credit spreads have started to widen out."

Energy:

Oil extended gains, as the Middle East conflict widened with Houthi militants in Yemen threatening shipping through the Red Sea, ANZ Research analysts said.

Along with their ability to harass commercial shipping traffic, "operationally, the Houthis' most meaningful leverage is their ability to threaten Saudi Arabia's Yanbu export hub on the Red Sea-where the East-West Pipeline terminates," J.P. Morgan analysts said.

"Taken together, these risks could erode Riyadh's ability to bypass the Strait of Hormuz," the analysts said, with that increased risk potentially adding $20 a barrel to the price of oil.

Metals:

Gold and silver have "come under considerable pressure as the Middle East war triggered a broad macroeconomic shock across global markets, forcing investors to simultaneously reprice inflation, rates, growth and liquidity conditions," Saxo Bank's Ole Hansen said.

"Investors appear reluctant to re-engage with the longer-term hard-asset narrative until both macro conditions stabilize and the technical picture turns more supportive," he said

-

Iron ore strengthened in Asian trading. Supply constraints are intensifying due to rising fuel costs and the hurricane season, providing near-term support for the black metal, Nanhua Futures analysts said. Given the high volatility, the analysts advised a patient "wait-and-see" approach.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

Trump Weighs Military Operation to Extract Iran's Uranium

WASHINGTON-President Trump is weighing a military operation to extract nearly 1,000 pounds of uranium from Iran, according to U.S. officials, a complex and risky mission that would likely put American forces inside the country for days or longer.

Trump hasn't made a decision on whether to give the order, the officials said, adding that he is considering the danger to U.S. troops. But the president remains generally open to the idea, according to the officials, because it could help accomplish his central goal of preventing Iran from ever making a nuclear weapon.

Fed Officials Signal That Rate Cuts May Be Over

Officially, Federal Reserve policymakers are still projecting interest-rate cuts later this year.

But listen carefully, and a different message emerges. With inflation elevated by tariffs and oil, and the labor market soft but not collapsing, some officials are hinting that their next move could be either higher or lower.

Iran's Wealth Is Parked on London's Billionaires' Row

On a leafy residential stretch of North London dubbed Billionaires' Row, Saudi royals and wealthy Russians have long come shopping for high-end real estate. The Iranians were a more surprising addition to the neighborhood.

Several sprawling mansion plots along the thoroughfare more formally known as The Bishops Avenue make up a chunk of the extensive London property holdings funded by Iranian money that Western officials have linked to the country's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.

Why Russia Is Stepping Up Its Support for an Embattled Iran

Russia has emerged as one of the early winners in the Iran war, as surging oil prices have given its slumping economy a boost and the Trump administration has eased restrictions on Russian oil. But over the longer term, the conflict poses a much bigger threat to its global ambitions.

In recent weeks, Russia has stepped up its support for Iran, its closest partner in the Middle East, providing satellite imagery and drone technology to help Iran target U.S. forces in the region.

The Sudden Fall of OpenAI's Most Hyped Product Since ChatGPT

When Sam Altman arrived in Los Angeles to attend Vanity Fair's Oscar afterparty earlier this month, his company was just weeks away from being able to license its Sora video-generation tools to Hollywood studios.

After the smash success of ChatGPT, Sora was hyped as AI's next consumer-friendly frontier-a simple app that allowed users to put themselves and their friends in whatever video settings they choose, from dribbling like the Harlem Globetrotters to clashing lightsabers with Darth Vader.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

Expected Major Events for Monday

04:30/NED: Feb Producer confidence survey

04:30/NED: Mar Producer confidence survey

04:30/NED: Feb PPI

06:00/SWE: Feb Retail sales

06:30/HUN: Feb Trade Balance

07:00/SPN: Feb Retail Sales

07:00/SVK: Mar Economic sentiment indicator

07:00/SVK: Mar Business tendency survey

07:00/SWI: Mar KOF economic barometer

08:00/ITA: Feb PPI

08:00/BUL: Feb PPI

08:00/GER: Mar Bavaria CPI

08:00/GER: Mar North Rhine Westphalia CPI

08:00/GER: Mar Saxony CPI

08:00/GER: Mar Brandenburg CPI

08:00/GER: Mar Hesse CPI

08:00/GER: Mar Baden-Wuerttemberg CPI

08:30/UK: Feb Monetary & Financial Statistics

08:30/UK: Feb Bank of England effective interest rates

08:30/UK: Feb Money and Credit - Lending to Individuals, Lending to Businesses, Broad Money and Credit

09:00/EU: Mar Business & Consumer Surveys - Business Climate Indicator & Economic Sentiment Indicator

09:00/CYP: Jan Industrial Production Index

09:00/GRE: Feb PPI

09:00/CRO: Feb Retail trade

09:30/BEL: Mar CPI

12:00/GER: Mar Provisional CPI

17:59/UK: REC JobsOutlook survey

17:59/UK: Feb Adzuna UK Job Market Report

23:01/UK: Mar Shop Price Monitor

All times in GMT. Powered by Onclusive and Dow Jones.

Write to us at newsletters@dowjones.com

We offer an enhanced version of this briefing that is optimized for viewing on mobile devices and sent directly to your email inbox. If you would like to sign up, please go to https://newsplus.wsj.com/subscriptions.

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  March 30, 2026 00:22 ET (04:22 GMT)

data.title: Dow Jones Top Financial Services Headlines at 12 AM ET: Three Reasons the Stock Market Can Endure the War | Private ...
data.description:
Three Reasons the Stock Market Can Endure the War

So far the fall in share prices has been small given the scale of disruption. Here are some of the supports keeping them aloft.

----

Private Credit's Exposure to Ailing Software Industry Is Bigger Than Advertised

Analysis by The Wall Street Journal finds four of the largest private-credit funds have more exposure to the software industry than their filings suggest.

----

Is Another Financial Crisis Lurking in Private Credit?

It Is fast-growing, opaque and intertwined with banks but lacks the scale and leverage that cashiered the economy in 2007.

----

Battered by Stock Losses, Investors Find Little Relief in Bonds

Inflation fears and forced selling have led to a sharp increase in Treasury yields.

----

Market Dive Points to Wall Street's Growing Alarm Over Iran War

The selloff pulled the S&P 500 down for a fifth straight week and dragged the Dow and Nasdaq into correction.

----

FDIC Prepares Private Equity for Bigger Role in Next Bank-Failure Wave

The agency reduced the requirements for buyout firms that back troubled banks.

----

Fed's Barkin Says Iran War Is Raising Economic Uncertainty

The Richmond Fed president said holding interest rates steady in March was the right move amid a long stretch of elevated uncertainty.

----

Newsom Bans California Officials From Using Inside Information on Betting Platforms

The order followed reports of well-timed prediction-market wagers on world events affected by Trump administration actions.

----

Bank of America Agrees to Pay $72.5 Million to Settle Epstein Lawsuit

Leon Black used Bank of America while paying Epstein $170 million, according to the lawsuit.

----

Advent Weighing Expansion Routes for Share-Registry Provider

Advent International is in talks to expand Australian share-registry provider Automic overseas, with the U.S. private-equity firm weighing options including potential acquisitions.

----

CrossCountry Mortgage to Acquire Two Harbors

Two Harbors Investment accepted an improved acquisition bid from CrossCountry Mortgage, backing out of its previous merger agreement with UWM Holdings.

----

FTSE Russell Lowers Free-Float Minimum in U.K. for International Firms

The change is intended to make the indices more representative of the real economic exposure they are designed to measure, the index provider said.

----

Santander Flags Strong First Quarter, Sticks to Targets

The bank backed its targets despite increasing global economic uncertainty, with Chair Ana Botin saying its geographic diversification mitigates risk by reducing volatility.

----

The Private-Credit Industry's Trouble: Surging Redemptions, Slower Fundraising

Investors are debating what the data shows about the health of private credit. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  March 30, 2026 00:15 ET (04:15 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Energy Headlines at 12 AM ET: U.S. oil prices turn higher for the week as Trump's 10-day pause fails to calm Iran war concerns | How ...
data.description:
U.S. oil prices turn higher for the week as Trump's 10-day pause fails to calm Iran war concerns

Oil prices strengthened Friday, leading U.S. benchmark crude to turn higher for the week, as President Trump's latest pause on striking Iran's energy infrastructure did little to calm nerves over tightening global supplies.

----

How Trump Media Joined the Nuclear Industry's Quest to Create a Star on Earth

Fusion company TAE Technologies was facing a funding crunch last year when it found an unlikely partner.

----

U.S. Requires Gas and Diesel Contain More Biofuels Made From Crops

In a win for farmers, the EPA increased the amount of biofuels that must be blended into gas and diesel.

----

Major Australian LNG Plants Suffer Outages as Cyclone Hits

A strong cyclone has affected liquefied natural gas plants in Australia, potentially adding more pressure to a market already reeling from massive supply disruption caused by war in the Middle East.

----

Court Hands Argentina's Milei a Victory, Rejecting $16 Billion Verdict

The Argentine government was sued by shareholders for its 2012 takeover of the oil company YPF.

----

BASF Sells $291 Million Stake in Harbour Energy

The shares were sold at 273 pence a share, which is a 9% discount to Thursday's closing price of 300 pence a share.

----

Energy-Price Shock Hits a World Already Buried in Debt

World governments had more than $100 trillion in public debt when the Iran war began-limiting their ability to cushion the effects of energy-price shocks.

----

This map shows a crude ticking time bomb that hits much of the world's oil supply in April

Disruptions in the flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz over the past four weeks will unleash a "sequential" shock to global supplies that'll run from east to west with much of the world taking a hit by April, according to strategists at J.P. Morgan.

----

Energy Capital Partners Proceeds With Caution on Gas-Fired Power Plant Deals

ECP hunts for new investments in electricity-generating stations after selling Calpine, but rising asset values pose risks.

----

Trump Says the Energy Shock Will Be Short-Lived. CEOs Paint a Scarier Picture.

Some executives are privately expressing frustration with the administration's optimistic messaging, saying the disruption is already far-reaching.

----

Think Gas Prices Are High Now? Brace for More Sticker Shock.

GasBuddy's live price-tracker says the average gas price nationwide is $3.983 a gallon. That's $1.01 higher than last month as fuel prices continue to climb.

----

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Rise for Fifth Straight Week

Commercial crude oil stocks increased by 6.9 million barrels to 456.2 million barrels last week amid a drop in exports. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  March 30, 2026 00:15 ET (04:15 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: BYD's Shares Mixed After Weaker-Than-Expected Results
data.description:
By Jiahui Huang 




  BYD's shares were mixed in Hong Kong and Shenzhen after the company reported weaker-than-expected results for 2025, reflecting a challenging period for the automaker. 



  Its Hong Kong-listed shares fell 0.5% to 105.90 Hong Kong dollars, equivalent to US$13.61, early Monday, while its Shenzhen-listed shares were flat at 105.28 yuan. 



  Net profit fell 19% to 32.62 billion yuan for 2025, marking its first annual decline in four years, according to earnings released late Friday. Revenue rose 3.5% to 803.96 billion yuan for the period. 



  Fourth-quarter revenue dropped 14% to 237.7 billion yuan, while net profit for the quarter fell to 9.29 billion yuan, missing Visible Alpha analysts' estimate of 12.29 billion yuan. 



  The Chinese auto giant, known for its wide range of low-cost EVs, is experiencing a sales slowdown as domestic demand softens. Its market share in China has shrunk amid fierce competition and rapid new-model introductions. 



  The carmaker has faced pressure from Chinese authorities to halt a price war in the world's largest auto market, which has eroded profitability across the crowded EV sector. Its margin fell to 17.74% from 19.44% a year earlier. 



  The weaker-than-expected results highlight the difficult balancing act for BYD as it pursues global expansion while grappling with weakening profitability in China. 



  "For multiple reasons, including the competition strategies of BYD and peers, as well as the policy environment change with anti-involution approach, the company had a tough year in 2025," Nomura analysts said in a note. 



  BYD's EV market share in China slid to 24.6% in the fourth quarter from 33% a year earlier, due to a muted product cycle and intensified competition, Bernstein analysts wrote in a note. 



  The automaker hopes new technology will entice customers. In March, BYD released a new EV battery that can be fully charged in nine minutes using its flash-charging stations. The battery can reach 70% capacity from 10% in five minutes. 



  Some of BYD's midrange and high-end flagship models will be equipped with the new battery. Among them, the newly released Denza Z9 GT can travel up to about 640 miles on a single charge, the longest range among cars using the battery. 



  Though analysts at Bernstein see BYD facing hurdles in the first quarter, including weaker volumes and higher material costs, subsequent periods could bring further upside. 



  "Growth momentum should improve from the second quarter on new flash-charging model launches, continued overseas expansion and energy-storage system growth," they said. 



  Given subdued auto and EV sales in China in January and February, Nomura expects BYD may see more evident business improvement starting in the second quarter. 





  Write to Jiahui Huang at jiahui.huang@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  March 29, 2026 23:45 ET (03:45 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.description:
Eli Lilly, InSilico Strike AI Drug Discovery Deal

Hong Kong-listed InSilico sad the deal could be valued at as much as $2.75 billion.

----

The Sudden Fall of OpenAI's Most Hyped Product Since ChatGPT

Sam Altman hoped Sora would turn OpenAI into a creative pioneer. Instead, it looks like an expensive strategic miscalculation.

----

Food Megamergers Hardly Ever Work. Could McCormick-Unilever Be Different?

Combining with Unilever's food division would remake McCormick into a global powerhouse, but the recent history of Big Food mergers hits a cautionary note.

Politico Picks an Insider as Its New Editor in Chief

The publisher named executive Jonathan Greenberger to succeed co-founder and longtime Editor in Chief John Harris.

----

Silicon Valley Has Stopped Talking Politics-Except for This Google Executive

AI pioneer Jeff Dean is a rare tech leader who has been publicly criticizing actions by the Trump administration.

----

Ahead of Its Mega-IPO, SpaceX Reminds Investors Disruption Is Coming

SpaceX is planning to bring high-speed data direct from the stars to your phone.

----

Nike's stock is at 9-year lows ahead of earnings. It faces these questions as doubt grows over its turnaround.

The sportswear giant has been trying to focus more on the needs of athletes, but analysts say new products aren't catching on.

----

How Trump Media Joined the Nuclear Industry's Quest to Create a Star on Earth

Fusion company TAE Technologies was facing a funding crunch last year when it found an unlikely partner.

----

Bank of America Agrees to Pay $72.5 Million to Settle Epstein Lawsuit

Leon Black used Bank of America while paying Epstein $170 million, according to the lawsuit.

----

Novartis to Buy Excellergy for Up to $2 Billion

The acquisition brings a new approach to treating allergic diseases to the company's immunology pipeline.

----

CrossCountry Mortgage to Acquire Two Harbors

Two Harbors Investment accepted an improved acquisition bid from CrossCountry Mortgage, backing out of its previous merger agreement with UWM Holdings.

----

Mitsubishi Electric, Rohm, Toshiba to Explore Merger of Power Chip Businesses

The Japanese companies are exploring a possible merger of their power semiconductor businesses to cope with increasingly fierce international competition.

----

Johnson & Johnson Bankruptcy Foe Ousted in Talc Litigation

Johnson & Johnson failed for years to resolve its talc liabilities in chapter 11, but is now gaining ground outside of bankruptcy court by attacking the ethics of its leading mass-tort adversary.

----

Carnival Cuts Profit Outlook as Higher Fuel Costs Offset Resilient Demand

Carnival cut its full-year profit outlook, saying a spike in fuel costs will more than offset record bookings and continually strong demand in the recent quarter.

----

Pernod Ricard in Talks to Combine With Jack Daniel's Maker Brown-Forman

The two companies are discussing a deal that is mostly stock and could be weeks away. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  March 29, 2026 23:15 ET (03:15 GMT)

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 11 PM ET: Oil Rises, Asian Equities Fall on Fears of Widening Middle East Conflict | Japan ...
data.description:
Oil Rises, Asian Equities Fall on Fears of Widening Middle East Conflict

Oil rose on fears of a widening Middle conflict that could lead to more supply disruptions while Asian equities fell on concerns that the war could slow global economic growth.

----

Japan Steps Up Yen Warnings as Mideast War Stokes Inflation Concerns

Bank of Japan Gov. Kazuo Ueda joined a growing chorus of officials pledging to monitor the yen closely, as the Middle East conflict continues to pressure the currency and stoke inflation concerns.

----

Three Reasons the Stock Market Can Endure the War

So far the fall in share prices has been small given the scale of disruption. Here are some of the supports keeping them aloft.

----

Investors have nowhere to hide as financial markets groan under the weight of the Iran conflict

Four weeks into the Iran conflict, global financial markets are starting to show some serious signs of strain.

----

Trump Weighs Military Operation to Extract Iran's Uranium

The president hasn't made a decision, U.S. officials said, as he considers the risk to U.S. troops.

----

Fed Officials Signal That Rate Cuts May Be Over

Policymakers suggest interest rates could go up or down. The most probable path may be no move at all.

----

Private Credit's Exposure to Ailing Software Industry Is Bigger Than Advertised

Analysis by The Wall Street Journal finds four of the largest private-credit funds have more exposure to the software industry than their filings suggest.

----

Is Another Financial Crisis Lurking in Private Credit?

It Is fast-growing, opaque and intertwined with banks but lacks the scale and leverage that cashiered the economy in 2007.

----

Oil Execs See Higher Prices Ahead-And a Boost to Renewables

CEOs at a major energy conference expressed concerns about the cost of the war in Iran.

----

Battered by Stock Losses, Investors Find Little Relief in Bonds

Inflation fears and forced selling have led to a sharp increase in Treasury yields.

----

The Other Markets Being Rattled by the Blockage of Hormuz

It isn't just oil and gas. Supply-chain disruptions are rippling through markets for fertilizer, semiconductors, packaged consumer products and cotton.

----

Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Jobs, Eurozone Inflation Data Could Reveal Middle East War Impact

U.S. jobs data and eurozone provisional inflation figures for March will attract attention in the coming week as data begins to give a picture of how the war in the Middle East has affected economies.

----

U.S. endures weakest Treasury auctions in over 3 years as anxiety around Iran war grows

The Iran conflict has investors second-guessing one of the world's crucial safe-haven assets. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  March 29, 2026 23:15 ET (03:15 GMT)

data.title: U.S. stock futures sink, oil prices surge as Iran war shows no signs of letting up
data.description:
By Mike Murphy

Rescue workers search a building in Tehran that housed the offices of the Doha-headquartered news network Al Araby TV on Sunday following a missile strike earlier in the day.

U.S. stock-index futures fell and oil prices surged again on Sunday, following sharp losses on Wall Street on Friday, as investors are waking up to the reality that the economic effects of the Iran war - now entering its fifth week - are likely to last longer than first expected.

Dow Jones Industrial Average futures (YM00) were off their session lows but still down more than 120 points, or 0.3%, late Sunday. S&P 500 (ES00) futures declined 0.2% and Nasdaq-100 futures (NQ00) were down about 0.3%.

West Texas Intermediate crude prices (CL.1) (CLK26), the U.S. benchmark, shot more than 2% higher, recently trading near $102 a barrel. Brent crude (BRN00) (BRNK26), the global benchmark, was up about 3%.

As a Marine Expeditionary Unit arrived in the Middle East - pushing the number of U.S. troops there to around 50,000 - Iran warned Sunday that it would "destroy" any American ground invasion. Meanwhile, U.S. and Israeli forces continued to bombard targets in Iran, including in the capital, Tehran, as the critical Strait of Hormuz remained effectively closed to tanker traffic. And on Saturday, Yemen-based Houthi rebels said they had entered the conflict, firing two missiles at Israel. That raises the threat of renewed missile attacks against shipping in the Red Sea.

Along with their ability to harass commercial shipping traffic, "operationally, the Houthis' most meaningful leverage is their ability to threaten Saudi Arabia's Yanbu export hub on the Red Sea - where the East-West (Petroline) pipeline terminates," J.P. Morgan analysts said in a note Sunday.

"Taken together, these risks could erode Riyadh's ability to bypass the Strait of Hormuz," the J.P. Morgan analysts said, with that increased risk potentially adding $20 a barrel to the price of oil. Houthi escalation "is becoming less a question of if than of when," they added.

Also on Sunday, Pakistan announced it will "host and facilitate" talks between the U.S. and Iran in the coming days, according to the Associated Press, following a meeting of regional powers. Still, the chances of a cease-fire in the near term seem remote. Trump told reporters Sunday night the U.S. is negotiating with Iran "directly and indirectly," but also raised the possibility of seizing Kharg Island, the heart of Iran's oil production, and the Wall Street Journal reported Trump is considering using ground troops to capture Iran's uranium.

"The Houthis' entering the theatre reshapes the battlefield. What looked contained now resembles a fracture line running through the entire energy complex," Stephen Innes, managing partner at SPI Asset Management, said in a Sunday note. "Oil is the truth serum. And the price is telling you that the Middle East risk premium is now just a step away from igniting a regional powder keg."

"What makes this episode more dangerous is not just the price of oil but the uncertainty about its duration," Innes added. "Markets can absorb a spike. They struggle with persistence. And right now, the Strait of Hormuz is no longer a background risk. It is the hinge on which global growth expectations are starting to swing."

On Friday, the Dow DJIA joined the Nasdaq COMP in correction territory, defined as a drop of at least 10% from a recent high. The Dow, S&P 500 and Nasdaq all closed at their lowest levels since last July, and have posted weekly declines for five straight weeks. And while the S&P 500 SPX has not entered correction territory yet, more than half of its industry sectors have. Of the index's 11 sectors, all but energy are in the red for March, according to FactSet data.

With oil prices soaring and stocks tumbling, U.S. investors have been left with seemingly nowhere to go, as precious metals (GC00) (SI00) and cryptocurrencies (BTCUSD) have also fallen, while Treasury yields pushed higher last week.

"There's really been no place to hide this month," George Cipolloni, a veteran portfolio manager, told MarketWatch last week. "You can't go to stocks, you can't go to bonds, even credit spreads have started to widen out."

Read more: Investors have nowhere to hide as financial markets groan under the weight of the Iran conflict

President Donald Trump's ability to reassure investors also appears to be waning. His extension last week of an ultimatum on attacking Iranian energy infrastructure did nothing to calm markets, and investors seem to be reacting less to his comments than to tangible news from the Persian Gulf. "The risk is that constant flip-flopping and headline fatigue is starting to seriously undermine the efficacy of the 'Trump put,'" Barclays analysts wrote in a note Friday.

U.S. gas prices on Sunday edged ever closer to averaging $4 a gallon, up about $1 a gallon from a month ago. The $4-a-gallon level will likely have a psychological effect on consumers, with ever-rising gas prices likely forcing some households to drive - and consume - less. That could have an effect on everything from vacations to trips to the grocery store.

See: Gas prices are nearing this 'psychological wall.' One group of drivers might smash right through.

This week, investors will also be looking at a number of earnings reports from consumer-focused companies, including Nike (NKE), Conagra Brands (CAG) and Dave and Buster's Entertainment (PLAY), which should shed more light on the state of consumer spending.

It'll be a short week, with markets closed for the Good Friday holiday, though the March jobs report will still be released Friday.

-Mike Murphy

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  03-29-26 2300ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

Of the index's 11 sectors, all but energy are in the red for March, according to FactSet data.

With oil prices soaring and stocks tumbling, U.S. investors have been left with seemingly nowhere to go, as precious metals (GC00) (SI00) and cryptocurrencies (BTCUSD) have also fallen, while Treasury yields pushed higher last week.

"There's really been no place to hide this month," George Cipolloni, a veteran portfolio manager, told MarketWatch last week. "You can't go to stocks, you can't go to bonds, even credit spreads have started to widen out."

Read more: Investors have nowhere to hide as financial markets groan under the weight of the Iran conflict

President Donald Trump's ability to reassure investors also appears to be waning. His extension last week of an ultimatum on attacking Iranian energy infrastructure did nothing to calm markets, and investors seem to be reacting less to his comments than to tangible news from the Persian Gulf. "The risk is that constant flip-flopping and headline fatigue is starting to seriously undermine the efficacy of the 'Trump put,'" Barclays analysts wrote in a note Friday.

U.S. gas prices on Sunday edged ever closer to averaging $4 a gallon, up about $1 a gallon from a month ago. The $4-a-gallon level will likely have a psychological effect on consumers, with ever-rising gas prices likely forcing some households to drive - and consume - less. That could have an effect on everything from vacations to trips to the grocery store.

See: Gas prices are nearing this 'psychological wall.' One group of drivers might smash right through.

This week, investors will also be looking at a number of earnings reports from consumer-focused companies, including Nike (NKE), Conagra Brands (CAG) and Dave and Buster's Entertainment (PLAY), which should shed more light on the state of consumer spending.

It'll be a short week, with markets closed for the Good Friday holiday, though the March jobs report will still be released Friday.

-Mike Murphy

  (END) Dow Jones Newswires

data.title: Australian Government Halves Fuel Excise Amid Price Surge
data.description:
By James Glynn 




  SYDNEY--The Australian government will sharply reduce federal taxes on road use and fuel costs to cushion the impact on consumers as gasoline prices surge amid the ongoing war in Iran. 



  Following a meeting in Canberra of federal, state and territory leaders, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese announced the so-called fuel excise would be cut in half for three months, while other charges currently levied on heavy transport will be scrapped for the same period. 



  The changes will cost the federal budget 2.55 billion Australian dollars, and will take effect Wednesday, the government said Monday. 



  Australians are dealing with steep increases in fuel costs across the country, with some economists warnings of a sharp hit to economic growth and a coming spike in inflation. 





  Write to James Glynn at james.glynn@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  March 29, 2026 22:38 ET (02:38 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Japan Steps Up Yen Warnings as Mideast War Stokes Inflation Concerns—Update
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa 




  TOKYO--Bank of Japan Gov. Kazuo Ueda joined a growing chorus of officials pledging to monitor the yen closely, as the Middle East conflict continues to pressure the currency and stoke inflation concerns. 



  "Exchange-rate fluctuations are one of the key factors that significantly affect our nation's economy and price conditions," Ueda told parliament on Monday. 



  The BOJ governor reiterated that the central bank is aware that currency swings are becoming more likely to influence prices, as Japanese companies grow more comfortable passing on higher costs to consumers. 



  "We should pay careful attention to the fact that [currency moves] could also affect underlying inflation through changes in inflation expectations," he said. 



  Also on Monday, Japan's top currency diplomat, Atsushi Mimura, intensified his rhetoric against speculative moves driving the yen's sharp decline. 



  He suggested the possibility of government operations to support the currency, which has recently stood near the 160 intervention danger zone against the dollar as safe-haven demand bolsters the greenback. 



  "There have been observations of increased speculative activity not only in the crude oil futures market but also in the foreign exchange market. If this persists, I believe we will soon need to take decisive action," Mimura said. 



  While the BOJ left its policy rate unchanged at 0.75% earlier this month, a summary of opinions from that meeting released Monday showed the policy board already on edge. 



  One member warned that the bank may be forced to speed up the pace of interest-rate hikes if geopolitical risks persist, the summary showed. 



  "With the policy interest rate still far away from the neutral interest rate, falling behind the curve would compel the bank to pursue rapid and significant monetary tightening, which would cause a major shock to Japan's economy," one BOJ board member said. 



  The BOJ's summary doesn't identify speakers. 



  In his parliamentary testimony, Ueda indicated that a steady policy normalization would ultimately help anchor the market. 



  "We believe that by raising short-term rates at an appropriate pace in line with the rising probability of reaching the 2% inflation target, long-term rates will be established stably and remain consistent with such policy actions," he said. 





  Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  March 29, 2026 22:26 ET (02:26 GMT)
